text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Consuming one egg a day accounted for <1% of CHD risk compared with 40% represented by all modifiable lifestyle risk factors.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"consuming one egg a day accounted for <1% of CHD risk compared with 40% represented by all modifiable lifestyle risk factors","entailment":"The quoted risk apportionment study directly reports the proportion of CHD risk attributable to one egg per day versus lifestyle factors, supporting the claim.","population":"US adults","intervention":"one egg per day","comparator":"all modifiable lifestyle risk factors","outcome":"CHD risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"<1% vs 40%","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Eggs do not raise TMAO levels.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"RCT which provided 2–6 eggs daily in healthy ... and at risk ... groups of participants confirmed that eggs do not raise TMAO levels","entailment":"The randomized controlled trials cited report that participants consuming 2–6 eggs daily showed no increase in TMAO, directly supporting the claim.","population":"healthy and at‑risk participants","intervention":"2–6 eggs daily","comparator":"","outcome":"TMAO levels","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Adding whole eggs or egg white to a glucose dietary challenge in prediabetic men attenuated post‑prandial oxidative stress.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Indeed, adding whole eggs or egg white to a glucose dietary challenge in prediabetic men attenuated post‑prandial oxidative stress","entailment":"The study result states that the addition of eggs reduced oxidative stress after a glucose challenge, directly supporting the claim.","population":"prediabetic men","intervention":"whole eggs or egg white added to glucose challenge","comparator":"","outcome":"post‑prandial oxidative stress","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"LDL‑c rise with egg feeding was attenuated in insulin‑resistant participants, regardless of obesity status.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"the LDL‑c rise with egg feeding was attenuated in insulin resistant participants, regardless of obesity status","entailment":"The double‑blind RCT reported that insulin‑resistant participants showed a smaller increase in LDL‑c when fed eggs, supporting the claim.","population":"insulin‑resistant participants","intervention":"egg feeding (zero, two and four egg yolks per day)","comparator":"","outcome":"LDL‑c rise","timeframe":"4 weeks","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Levels of LDL‑c were unchanged after 12 weeks in participants with T2D fed high‑egg diets.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"levels of LDL‑c were unchanged after 12 weeks and other CVD markers showed improvements, indicating that egg consumption was beneficial, not harmful, in these groups","entailment":"The cited RCTs on T2D participants report no change in LDL‑c after 12 weeks of high‑egg intake, directly supporting the claim.","population":"people with T2D","intervention":"high‑egg diets","comparator":"","outcome":"LDL‑c levels","timeframe":"12 weeks","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Eating 6–12 eggs per week did not significantly affect TC, LDL‑c, TG, fasting glucose, insulin or C‑reactive protein in people with prediabetes or T2D, while HDL‑c increased.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"eating 6–12 eggs/week did not significantly affect TC, LDL‑c, TG, fasting glucose, insulin or C‑reactive protein in people with prediabetes or T2D, while HDL‑c increased in several studies","entailment":"The systematic review of RCTs reports no significant changes in multiple cardiometabolic markers with 6–12 eggs weekly, supporting the claim.","population":"people with prediabetes or T2D","intervention":"6–12 eggs per week","comparator":"","outcome":"TC, LDL‑c, TG, fasting glucose, insulin, C‑reactive protein (no change); HDL‑c (increase)","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Approximately 15 g of egg white contains 1341 mg leucine.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"approximately 15 g of egg white contains 1341 mg leucine","entailment":"The statement provides a factual measurement of leucine content in egg white, directly supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"15 g egg white","comparator":"","outcome":"leucine content (1341 mg)","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"1341 mg","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"Eggs could be a familiar and low‑cost way to deliver high‑quality protein and micronutrients into the diets of older people.","claimType":"value","quote":"eggs could be a familiar and low-cost way to deliver high-quality protein and micronutrients into the diets of older people","entailment":"The authors suggest that eggs are an accessible, inexpensive source of quality protein and micronutrients for older adults, reflecting a value judgment.","population":"older people","intervention":"eggs","comparator":"","outcome":"delivery of high‑quality protein and micronutrients","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"normative"}]}
